content:
The Campaign That Worked Without a Website Visit
A marketing leader once knew how to read the dashboard.
Impressions meant reach. Clicks meant interest. Website visits meant opportunity. Conversions meant success. The path was not perfect, but it was familiar.
Then the dashboard began to tell a different story.
A customer asked an AI assistant, “What is the best laptop for a consultant who travels every week?” The assistant asked a few clarifying questions, compared battery life, weight, reviews, warranty, price, and delivery, then produced a shortlist. One brand appeared as “best overall.” Another appeared as “best budget option.” A third was not mentioned at all.

• The customer did not browse ten websites.

• The customer did not click on five ads.

• The customer did not compare endless tabs.

• The customer chose the answer.
For advertisers, this is the next major shift. The old digital advertising model was built around a visible journey: search, click, visit, convert. AI compresses that journey into a guided decision. The user may still have strong intent, but the click may no longer be the moment where value is created.
The ad does not disappear. It moves closer to the decision.
And when advertising moves closer to the decision, the business model changes.
The old ad asked for your click. The new ad may ask for your decision.

Why the Click Became So Powerful
The click became the foundation of digital advertising because it gave the market something traditional media could not easily provide: a measurable response.
A billboard could build awareness. A TV campaign could influence culture. A magazine ad could shape brand perception. But digital advertising gave executives a cleaner chain of evidence: the user saw the ad, clicked the ad, landed on a page, and took action.
That measurement changed everything.
Search ads became powerful because they appeared close to intent. Display ads were scaled because they could be tracked. Affiliate marketing grew because clicks could be attributed. Programmatic advertising expanded because impressions and actions could be bought and optimized at an enormous scale.
By 2025, U.S. digital advertising had reached nearly $300 billion, according to the IAB and PwC, with the industry continuing to shift toward performance-driven, AI-powered growth. [1]
But the click was always a convenient proxy, not the final truth.
A click could signal interest, but not commitment. It could be accidental, low-quality, duplicated, bot-generated, or disconnected from real purchase intent. It measured movement, not necessarily value.
AI search exposes that weakness.
If an assistant can understand the user’s need, compare options, filter irrelevant choices, and move the user closer to action, then advertisers will increasingly ask a hard question:
Why pay for a click when the platform may be able to deliver a qualified outcome?
The click was never the goal. It was the signal we used because we had nothing better.

The Ad Moves Inside the Answer
Traditional digital ads revolved around content and search results.
They appeared above links, beside articles, inside feeds, before videos, and across websites. Even when targeted, they were still mostly external to the user’s actual reasoning process. They interrupted, reminded, followed, or redirected.
AI changes the location of influence.
A user asks, “Which insurance plan is best for my family?” “Which project management tool should my team use?” “What is the best hotel near the conference venue?” “Which bank account is right for a small business?” “What should I buy for a four-year-old who loves art?”
These are not casual impressions. These are decision moments.
Google has already begun adapting to this reality. Its Ads documentation explains that ads can appear above, below, or within AI Overviews in eligible markets, and that both the user query and the AI Overview context can be considered when serving those ads. [2] Google has also announced new AI-era search ad formats built with Gemini, including ad experiences designed for conversational discovery and product guidance. [3]
This is more than a placement change. It is a change in advertising psychology.
An ad next to a search result is clearly commercial. A recommendation within an AI answer feels more like advice.
That is why the next ad surface is more powerful and more dangerous. If users believe they are receiving neutral guidance while commercial incentives shape the answer, trust will erode quickly.
When the answer becomes the ad surface, disclosure becomes part of the product.

From Cost Per Click to Cost Per Outcome
The logic of AI advertising points toward a new pricing model.
The old model asked: Did the user click? The new model will ask: Did the user act?
That does not mean pay-per-click disappears overnight. Search ads, display ads, marketplace ads, affiliate links, sponsored listings, and retargeting will continue. But their strategic center of gravity may shift as AI assistants take more responsibility for discovery, comparison, and action.
The next advertising economy may be built around outcomes such as verified leads, completed tasks, AI recommendations, completed transactions, and qualified conversations.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This shift is attractive because it moves advertising closer to business value. Advertisers have spent years paying for traffic that did not convert. AI platforms may promise fewer empty visits and more meaningful outcomes.
But the trade-off is serious.
The more platforms deliver outcomes, the more they control the path to those outcomes. They may shape the shortlist, manage the recommendation, control the checkout, and define the attribution.
That gives platforms enormous power over demand.
The next advertising economy will not ask only, “Did the user click?” It will ask, “Who shaped the decision?”

Sponsored Recommendations: The Most Delicate Format
Sponsored recommendations will likely become one of the most contested ad formats of the AI era.
The concept is simple: a user asks for help, the AI provides options, and a brand pays to appear in the recommendation environment. The placement may be a sponsored follow-up question, a promoted product card, a labeled recommendation, a preferred offer, or a transaction prompt.
There is nothing inherently wrong with this if it is transparent and relevant. A user looking for travel insurance, accounting software, running shoes, cloud hosting, or a hotel may appreciate sponsored options if they are clearly marked and genuinely useful.
The problem is that recommendations carry authority.
A sponsored search link says, “Click here.” A sponsored AI answer may imply, “Choose this.”
That is a much more sensitive form of persuasion.
Perplexity’s experience shows the tension. It was among the first AI answer engines to experiment with advertising, including sponsored answers or sponsored placements, but later reports said the company was moving away from ads because users need to trust that the answer is the best possible answer. [4]
That tension will define the market.
The more helpful an AI assistant becomes, the more damaging hidden commercial influence becomes. Users can tolerate ads. They may not tolerate advice that feels quietly bought.
A sponsored recommendation must therefore meet a higher standard than a banner ad. It should be clearly labeled, placed close to the recommendation, separated from organic guidance, and explain why it is relevant. If a recommendation is paid, the user should not need to hunt for the disclosure.
A sponsored answer without clear disclosure is not innovation. It is a manipulation with a better design.

AI Shopping Assistants and Embedded Commerce
Commerce is where the shift becomes most concrete.
AI shopping assistants are not only better search boxes. They are becoming decision engines. They can ask clarifying questions, compare products, evaluate reviews, filter by budget, check availability, and reduce the work of choosing.
OpenAI has introduced shopping research in ChatGPT, describing a product experience that helps users find the right products by asking clarifying questions, researching online, reviewing sources, and building a personalized buyer’s guide. [5] OpenAI has also expanded product discovery through the Agentic Commerce Protocol, allowing richer shopping experiences and more complete, up-to-date product information inside ChatGPT. [6]
The infrastructure is becoming more practical. OpenAI’s developer documentation says merchants can provide structured product feeds so ChatGPT can index and display products with current price and availability. The feed schema includes fields that control whether a product is discoverable in ChatGPT search results and whether it is eligible for checkout. [7]
That detail matters.
It shows that AI commerce is not only a front-end experience. It is becoming a data integration layer. Product feeds, pricing, reviews, images, availability, checkout eligibility, and seller context become the new advertising infrastructure.
The digital shelf is changing.
In physical retail, brands fought for shelf placement. In e-commerce, they fought for search ranking, marketplace placement, and sponsored listings. In AI commerce, they may fight for inclusion in the assistant’s shortlist.
If the AI recommends three products, the fourth may not exist in the customer’s mind.
The new shelf is not a webpage. It is the shortlist inside the assistant’s answer.

Amazon’s Signal: Ads Without Surrendering the Customer
A recent example captures the strategic tension.
Business Insider reported that Amazon began buying ads on ChatGPT, directing users back to Amazon’s storefront, while remaining protective of its own product, pricing, and inventory data. [8]
That is a small but important signal.
Amazon appears willing to use AI platforms as a channel for demand, but not necessarily to surrender the customer relationship, product data, or the transaction layer. In other words, Amazon may treat AI answer engines as a new source of intent but still wants the final purchase to happen on Amazon-controlled infrastructure.
This is the battle every major platform and retailer will face.

• Who owns the product data?

• Who owns the recommendation?

• Who owns the checkout?

• Who owns the customer relationship?

• Who owns the measurement?
In the click economy, traffic moved from search to site. In the AI outcome economy, platforms may seek to retain more of the journey within their own environment.
Not all retailers will accept that quietly.
Some will feed AI systems to gain visibility. Some will restrict access to protect margins and data. Some will buy ads inside AI platforms but route users back to their own storefronts. Some will build their own AI shopping assistants.
The future of advertising will therefore be shaped not only by ad formats, but by data negotiation.
In AI commerce, the fight is not only for attention. It is for controlling the transaction.

The Attribution Problem Gets Harder
Outcome-based advertising sounds cleaner than click-based advertising, but it creates a difficult question:
Who deserves credit when an AI assistant influences a purchase?
The answer may involve several parties. A publisher may provide the review. A brand may provide the product feed. A platform may generate the recommendation. A marketplace may complete the transaction. A payment provider may process the checkout. A retargeting campaign may have introduced the customer earlier.
The more compressed the journey becomes, the harder attribution becomes.
In traditional digital advertising, attribution was already imperfect. Last-click attribution over-rewarded the final touch. Multi-touch attribution was often model-driven and disputed. Privacy changes made tracking harder. Walled gardens marked their own homework.
AI adds a new layer: conversational influence.
A user may ask an AI assistant for advice on Monday, compare
